title: Painting a ceiling
subtitle: Cut in at the cornice, then roll the main field without lap marks
excerpt: How to prepare and paint a domestic ceiling: clearing and protecting the room, applying a mist coat over bare plaster if needed, cutting in the perimeter with a brush, and rolling the main field in parallel bands to avoid lap marks and a patchy finish.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). City and Guilds Level 2 Painting and Decorating syllabus.

content.text: A ceiling takes more preparation than a wall because every imperfection shows from below. Cracks catch shadows; blemishes are at eye level when the room is lit from above. The job divides into three phases: preparation,
the perimeter with a brush, and rolling the main field. Allow each coat to dry completely before applying the next.

Remove or cover all furniture with dust sheets. Lay a cotton dust sheet on the floor: paint drops from a ceiling roller drop constantly. Mask the tops of the walls where they meet the ceiling if painting the ceiling a different colour from the walls; painter's tape set on a straight line saves repainting the wall edge. If the light fitting is staying in place, drape it with a small plastic bag and tape it at the base.

Fill any cracks or nail holes with lightweight filler. Allow to cure fully. Sand smooth with 120-grit on a sanding pole. Wipe the ceiling with a barely damp cloth to remove dust. Check for any flaking paint or loose surface; lift it with a scraper and feather the edges with the filling knife. If the ceiling is newly plastered or bare, apply a
first (see the companion technique tutorial on bare plaster preparation).

Load the 50 mm brush with paint (dip one third of the bristle length, tap off the excess; do not wipe the brush on the rim). Cut a band about 75 mm wide around the entire perimeter of the ceiling where it meets the wall or cornice. Keep the cut-in band wet while working; if it dries before the roller reaches it, a lap mark will form at the junction. On a single room, cut in the complete perimeter before loading the roller.

Load the roller in the tray: roll it several times through the paint until the sleeve is uniformly loaded without excess. Apply the first band of paint parallel to the shorter dimension of the room, working in strips about 600 mm wide. Overlap each strip into the previous wet edge immediately (do not allow the previous strip to dry before adding the next). Maintaining this wet edge prevents
. Work from one end of the room to the other in parallel bands, then lightly roll the whole ceiling in one direction (with the light from the window) to unify the texture.

Allow the first coat to dry completely (typically 2 hours in a ventilated room at 18 to 22 degrees; do not judge by touch in the centre of the room where it stays wetter for longer). Apply the second coat at 90 degrees to the first. Two coats of a good matt ceiling paint give full opacity and a flat, even surface.
Apply a mist coat of diluted emulsion before the finish coats. A mist coat applied straight-to-plaster means the first finish coat will streak, even with two full-strength coats to follow.
Seal the stain first with a stain-blocking primer (shellac-based for stubborn stains). Standard emulsion over an untreated water stain bleeds through every coat.

Use a thick-pile roller sleeve (15 mm nap) to get paint into the texture. Pre-1990s textured coatings may contain asbestos fibres: do not sand, scrape, or disturb them. Painting over is safe; disturbing them is not.

attrs.fix: Sand back lightly and repaint, maintaining a continuous wet edge across the whole ceiling without stopping mid-section. In large rooms, work with a second person so one cuts in while the other rolls.
attrs.cause: The wet edge was lost: one strip dried before the next strip was applied alongside it.
attrs.symptom: Lap marks visible under raking light after the paint dries

attrs.fix: Apply a third coat at 90 degrees to the previous coat, keeping even load on the roller throughout.
attrs.cause: The roller was not consistently loaded: dry areas were applied over wet ones mid-roll.
attrs.symptom: Paint finish is patchy, opaque in some areas, thin in others

attrs.fix: Strip the loose paint, prepare the surface thoroughly, and start again with a mist coat.
attrs.cause: Paint applied over dusty, greasy, or damp plaster. The bond never formed properly.
attrs.symptom: Paint flakes from the surface within weeks
